coredata.dc:title: Starch/carboxymethyl cellulose-based photodynamic packaging material delays the quality deterioration of snap beans via ROS metabolism regulation
coredata.dc:creator: Gong, Dezhuang
Zhang, Xinhua
Zhang, Jing
Mu, Jingyi
Han, Xiaofeng
Zhang, Xiuling
Zhang, Wentao
coredata.dc:description: Snap beans (Phaseolus vulgaris L.) are prone to rapid senescence and quality deterioration during postharvest storage. In this study, the effect of a novel carbon dot/g-C3N4-mediated starch/carboxymethyl cellulose-based photodynamic film (CCPF) on the postharvest quality deterioration and reactive oxygen species (ROS) metabolism of snap bean pods was investigated. CCPF packaging influenced gas composition regulation, ethylene degradation and microbial infection, which resulted in snap bean pods with lower respiratory metabolism and less quality loss. In addition, the snap bean pods in the CCPF group maintained the content of nonenzymatic antioxidants, low activities of pro-oxidative enzymes and high activities of antioxidant enzymes compared to other groups, thus decreasing electrolyte leakage, malondialdehyde accumulation, H2O2 accumulation and the O2·- generation rate. Overall, CCPF packaging effectively delayed postharvest senescence and extended the shelf life of snap beans by reducing respiration and regulating ROS metabolism. Pearson’s correlation analysis confirmed that postharvest senescence correlated with ROS metabolism in snap bean pods. This study offers theoretical evidence for a method to preserve snap bean pods and can be used as a valuable reference for the practical application of novel CCPF packaging.

The respiration rate (A), weight loss (B) and firmness (C) of snap bean pods packaged with pure corn starch/carboxymethyl cellulose film and carbon dot/g-C3N4 nanocomposite-mediated corn starch/carboxymethyl cellulose photodynamic film during storage for 15 d. Vertical bars represent the standard deviation of means. Different lowercase letters mean statistically significant difference.

Visual appearance of snap beans packaged with pure corn starch/carboxymethyl cellulose film and carbon dot/g-C3N4 nanocomposite-mediated corn starch/carboxymethyl cellulose photodynamic film compared with control during storage for 15 d.

Microstructure of fresh snap bean pods (A); Microstructure of snap bean pods packaged with pure corn starch/carboxymethyl cellulose film (C) and carbon dot/g-C3N4 nanocomposite-mediated corn starch/carboxymethyl cellulose photodynamic film (D) compared with control (B) after storage for 15 d.

The total soluble solids content (A), titratable acidity (B), soluble protein content (C) and total chlorophyll content (D) of snap bean pods with different treatments during storage for 15 d. Vertical bars represent the standard deviation of means. Different lowercase letters mean statistically significant difference.

The relative conductivity (A), malondialdehyde (MDA) content (B), O2·- generation rate (C) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) content (D) of snap bean pods with different treatments during storage for 15 d. Vertical bars represent the standard deviation of means. Different lowercase letters mean statistically significant difference.

The peroxidase (POD) activity (A), polyphenol oxidase (PPO) activity (B), superoxide dismutase (SOD) activity (C), catalase (CAT) activity (D), ascorbate peroxidase (APX) activity (E) and glutathione reductase (GR) activity (F) of snap bean pods with different treatments during storage for 15 d. Vertical bars represent the standard deviation of means. Different lowercase letters mean statistically significant difference.

The ascorbic acid (AsA) content (A) and glutathione (GSH) content (B) of snap bean pods with different treatments during storage for 15 d. Vertical bars represent the standard deviation of means. Different lowercase letters mean statistically significant difference.

(A) Heatmap of Pearson’s correlations based on the measured parameters in snap bean pods after 15 d of storage. Asterisk (*) indicates a significant correlation at p < 0.05, and a double asterisk (**) indicates a significant correlation at p < 0.01. Red presents a positive correlation, and blue presents a negative correlation. (B) Hierarchical cluster analysis for the quality of snap bean pods after different packaging treatments during the storage. FS indicates fresh samples, and 3, 6, 9, 12 and 15 present storage days.

The possible mechanism of the photodynamic film packaging treatment delaying the postharvest senescence and maintaining the overall quality of snap bean pods via regulating the reactive oxygen species (ROS) metabolism. Red arrows represent promotive effects; green arrows represent inhibitory effects.

head.ce:title: Starch/carboxymethyl cellulose-based photodynamic packaging material delays the quality deterioration of snap beans via ROS metabolism regulation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Snap beans (Phaseolus vulgaris L.) are prone to rapid senescence and quality deterioration during postharvest storage. In this study, the effect of a novel carbon dot/g-C3N4-mediated starch/carboxymethyl cellulose-based photodynamic film (CCPF) on the postharvest quality deterioration and reactive oxygen species (ROS) metabolism of snap bean pods was investigated. CCPF packaging influenced gas composition regulation, ethylene degradation and microbial infection, which resulted in snap bean pods with lower respiratory metabolism and less quality loss. In addition, the snap bean pods in the CCPF group maintained the content of nonenzymatic antioxidants, low activities of pro-oxidative enzymes and high activities of antioxidant enzymes compared to other groups, thus decreasing electrolyte leakage, malondialdehyde accumulation, H2O2 accumulation and the O2·- generation rate. Overall, CCPF packaging effectively delayed postharvest senescence and extended the shelf life of snap beans by reducing respiration and regulating ROS metabolism. Pearson’s correlation analysis confirmed that postharvest senescence correlated with ROS metabolism in snap bean pods. This study offers theoretical evidence for a method to preserve snap bean pods and can be used as a valuable reference for the practical application of novel CCPF packaging.

ce:section.ce:para: The snap bean (Phaseolus vulgaris L.), also known as “green bean”, is an economically important crop with high nutritional and edible value in the family Leguminosae (Wang et al., 2022). Snap bean pods, which are usually consumed fresh, are rich in nutrients, contain various trace elements and essential amino acids, and have good antioxidant, anti-obesity, lipid-lowering, and immunomodulatory effects, among other biological activities (Jannat et al., 2022). However, snap bean quality rapidly deteriorates after harvesting due to strong physiological activities, including stress associated with storage conditions and infection by pathogenic agents (El-Garhy et al., 2016). In addition, the postharvest senescence of snap beans is associated with reactive oxygen species (ROS)-mediated oxidative damage. The production of ROS is affected by various biotic and abiotic stress factors. As abiotic stress, postharvest storage may lead to increased ROS production, decreased free water and interference with electron transfer in the mitochondria (Li et al., 2013). Oxidative damage caused by excessive accumulation of ROS can induce oxidative stress, which leads to membrane lipid peroxidation, protein degradation, lipid damage and the inhibition of enzyme activity, thus adversely affecting the storage life and commercial value of snap beans (Fang et al., 2016).

Active nanocomposite packaging is believed to be a feasible and convenient strategy for maintaining snap bean freshness. Numerous studies have reported that various active composite films can regulate ROS metabolism and extend the shelf life of postharvest foods (Yu et al., 2022; Feng et al., 2022; Fang et al., 2016). For example, Feng et al. (2022) packaged Agaricus bisporus with bioactive films loaded with melatonin and found that these films effectively removed excess reactive oxygen species and reduced the damage to membrane lipids, thereby extending shelf life. Recently, various light-responsive active packaging systems have been studied and applied in the food preservation field due to their advantages of controllable properties and sustainable functional activity (Ni et al., 2021; Ezati et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2022; Duan et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021; Wen et al., 2023). These photodynamic active films use photosensitizers and appropriate excitation sources to produce reactive oxygen species (ROS), which enable the inhibition of bacteria and the degradation of ethylene (Ni et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2019). Many studies have also reported photodynamic inactivation (PDI) is a reliable and innovative antimicrobial treatment method with mild conditions and negligible damage to normal tissue cells (Wen et al., 2023; Li et al., 2020; Mao et al., 2019). Previously, our lab successfully developed a CS/CMC-based nanocomposite packaging film embedded with a metal-free carbon dot/g-C3N4 (CN) photocatalyst and confirmed that this photodynamic nanocomposite film possessed excellent mechanical strength, water resistance, barrier properties and biosafety and exhibited satisfactory antibacterial performance under visible light, thus demonstrating the preservation potential for snap beans. However, the effect of photodynamic packaging on postharvest preservation and ROS metabolism in snap bean pods remains unclear.

Therefore, the aim of this study was to explore the effect of a novel CS/CMC-based photodynamic film on the postharvest quality of snap bean pods. From the perspective of ROS metabolism, the mechanism by which photodynamic packaging treatment preserves snap bean pods was clarified by investigating related enzyme activities, membrane lipid peroxidation and antioxidation systems. In addition, the relationship between snap bean pod senescence and ROS metabolism was analyzed. This work provides a promising strategy for delaying senescence in snap beans, providing insight into the mechanism of preserving snap bean pods, and lays the foundation for broadening the application of CS/CMC-based photodynamic films.

Fresh snap bean samples (Phaseolus vulgaris L.) were harvested from Heilongjiang Youdoujiao Technology Development Co., Ltd. (Harbin, China) and immediately transferred to the laboratory. CS and CMC were obtained from Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

The preparation of carbon dot/g-C3N4 (CN) nanocomposite-mediated CS/CMC photodynamic film (CCPF) was carried out based on a previous study by our lab (Gong et al., 2023). The CN nanocomposites were made up of 500 mg g-C3N4 and 100 mg citrus peel-derived carbon dots (CPCDs) which were mixed with 4.5 g of CS, 0.5 g of CMC and 1.75 g of glycerin (plasticizer) before being heated at 85 °C for 30 min to make a paste of film-forming solution. After this, 15 mL of the film-forming solution was cast onto plastic Petri dishes and dried in an oven at 50 °C, resulting in the final CCPF. Pure CS/CMC film (CCF) was prepared using a similar procedure without adding CN nanocomposites. Snap beans with uniform color and maturity, that were undamaged and disease-free were equally allocated into three groups and packaged with pure CCF (CCF group) and CCPF (CCPF group); snap beans with no treatment were used as a control (unpackaged group). Each packaging bag contained 175 ± 5 g of snap beans, and all samples were stored at 9 °C and 90% RH, followed by exposure to visible light (300 W Xenon lamp) for 1 h at 2, 5, 8, 11 and 14 d. The packaging materials used for the snap bean pods are shown in Fig. S1. Three samples were randomly selected from each group at 3 d intervals to measure relevant indicators.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Gas composition analysis inside different packages
ce:section.ce:para: The concentrations of oxygen (O2) and carbon dioxide (CO2) inside the packages were monitored every three days with an O2/CO2 analyzer (SCY-2A, Shanghai Xinrui, Shanghai, China). The ethylene concentration was determined using an Agilent 7890B gas chromatograph (Agilent, California, USA) equipped with a flame ionization detector (FID). The gas sample was extracted from the packaging bags with a needle, and the result was expressed as μL L-1.

The photocatalytic degradation of ethylene was measured and evaluated using the method reported by Xie et al. (2020) with minor modifications. In brief, the prepared nanocomposite film was placed in a glass reactor (2.0 L) covered with quartz glass. High-purity ethylene gas (initial concentration of about 0.15 mg/L) was injected into the reactor. Static adsorption was performed for three hours under dark conditions to establish absorption–desorption equilibrium before the sample was exposed to visible light (300 W xenon lamp, 420 < λ < 780 nm) for ethylene photodegradation. As the reaction proceeded, gas was extracted at 30 min intervals with a sampling needle and detected using an Agilent 7890B gas chromatograph (Agilent, California, USA) to obtain the ethylene concentration.

The respiration rate of snap bean samples was measured using the method reported by Huang et al. (2022) with minor modifications. Snap beans were randomly selected from each group and placed in an airtight container containing a Petri dish, and 10 mL of NaOH solution (0.4 mol L-1) was added to the Petri dish beforehand. After standing for 2 h, the remaining NaOH solution was titrated with 0.2 mol L-1 oxalic acid, and the volume of oxalic acid lost by titration was recorded. The respiration rate was calculated using the titrated amount of oxalic acid and expressed as CO2 mg kg-1 h-1 on a fresh weight basis.

The weight loss (WL) of snap beans was measured at each sampling interval, and the result was expressed as a percentage (%) using the following formula: WL (%) = (Initial weight of the snap beans - Weight of the snap beans on the sampling date)/Initial weight of the snap beans × 100. The firmness of snap bean pods was determined by a texture analyzer (TA-XT Plus, Stable Micro Systems, UK) equipped with a penetration probe. Three positions on the surface of the snap bean pods were selected for probe penetration. The maximum penetration force was recorded from three samples per replicate and expressed as Newtons (N). The microstructures of the snap bean pod samples were observed with an S-3400 N scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi, Japan). The snap bean pod tissues (4 mm × 2 mm) were fixed with glutaraldehyde, dehydrated with 50%, 70%, 90% and 100% ethanol, and then frozen and sprayed with gold for observation.

The total soluble solids (TSS) content of snap bean pods was measured with an LH-Q32 portable refractometer (Luheng, Zhejiang, China), as described by Jiang et al. (2022), with values expressed as a percentage (%). Titratable acidity (TA) was determined using the method described by Li et al. (2022) with minor modifications. Snap bean pod homogenate was diluted to 100 mL with deionized (DI) water and filtered after 20 min. The filtrate (10 mL) was titrated against NaOH solution, and the result was expressed as a percentage (%). The soluble protein content (SPC) was assessed by Coomassie Brilliant Blue (CBB) staining with bovine serum albumin (BSA) as the standard (Huang et al., 2022). The total chlorophyll content (TCC) of snap bean pods was assayed according to Li et al. (2022) with modification. The snap bean pod samples (1.0 g) were ground at 4 °C for 3 min in a mortar containing a small amount of quartz and 3 mL of 80% acetone. Subsequently, 10 mL of acetone was added to the homogenate and ground until the tissue appeared white. The resulting mixture was centrifuged at 8000g for 10 min at 4 °C, and the supernatant was diluted to a volume of 25 mL with 80% acetone. The absorbance of the solution was read at 663 and 645 nm on a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-2600, Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). TCC was assessed by Eq. (1) and expressed as g kg-1.
(1) TCC (g kg-1) = (20·29 × A 645 + 8·05 × A 663) × V/(1000 × m)
where A 645 and A 663 represent the absorbance values of the detection solution at 645 nm and 663 nm, respectively; V and m are the volume of the extracting solution (mL) and the weight of the snap bean pod samples (g), respectively.

Cell membrane permeability, expressed by RC, was assayed using a method by Min et al. (2018), with modification. One snap bean pod from each group was cut into ten pieces (diameter of 8 mm, thickness of 3 mm) by a hole punch and immersed in 20 mL DI water for 30 min. The initial electrical conductivity (Pi) was measured using a conductivity meter (Delta 326, Shanghai Leici, Shanghai, China). The above tissue disks were boiled for 15 min and then cooled to room temperature to determine the final electrical conductivity (Pf). The RC of the snap bean pods was calculated as follows:
(2) RC (%) = (Pf - Pi)/Pi × 100

The MDA content in snap bean pods was determined according to the thiobarbituric acid reaction method, as described by Huang et al. (2022). Snap bean pod samples (1.0 g) were homogenized with 5 mL trichloroacetic acid (10%, TCA) under ice bath conditions and centrifuged at 8000g for 15 min at 4 °C. Then, 2.0 mL of TBA (0.67%) solution was added to the supernatant (2.0 mL), mixed for 20 min at 100 °C, and cooled to room temperature. The absorbance of the mixture was recorded at 450, 532 and 600 nm. The result was expressed as mmol kg-1 after calculation using Eqs. (3) and (4).
(3) C (µmol L-1) = 6·45 × (A 532 - A 600) - 0·56 × A 450
(4) MDA content (μmol kg-1) = (C × Ve)/(Vs × m × 1000)
where A 450, A 532 and A 600 represent the absorbance values of the mixture at 450 nm, 532 nm and 600 nm, respectively; C represents the concentration of MDA in the reaction mixture; m is the weight of the test samples (g); Ve is the volume of the extracting buffer (mL); and Vs corresponds to the volume of the extracting buffer (mL) at the time of measurement.

The content of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) was determined as outlined by Li et al. (2022), with modification. The snap bean pod samples were homogenized and macerated in 5.0 mL precooled acetone solution and then centrifuged at 8000g for 15 min at 4 °C. One milliliter of the supernatant was removed and thoroughly mixed with titanium sulfate (0.1 mL), precooled acetone (1.0 mL) and concentrated ammonia water (0.2 mL). The solution was centrifuged and the separated precipitate was rinsed with acetone and then dissolved in 3 mL of 2 mol L-1 sulfuric acid. The absorbance of the solution was recorded at 412 nm after complete dissolution, and the result was expressed as mmol kg-1.

The superoxide anion (O2·-) generation rate was determined as described by Li et al. (2022), with modification. Snap bean pod tissues (1.0 g) were homogenized in 5.0 mL of sodium phosphate buffer (SPB, 50 mmol L-1, pH 7.8) containing polyvinyl-pyrrolidone (2%), ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (1.0 mmol L-1) and Triton X-100 (0.3%) and centrifuged at 8000g for 15 min at 4 °C. The supernatant of each sample (1.0 mL) was mixed with 1.0 mL SPB and 1.0 mL hydroxyl ammonium chloride (1.0 mmol L-1). The solution was thoroughly mixed and kept at 25 °C for 1 h. Afterward, 1.0 mL of p-aminobenzenesulfonic acid (17 mmol L-1) and α-naphthylamine (7 mmol L-1) were added to the above mixture and kept at 25 °C for 20 min. The absorbance of the solution that developed color was recorded at 530 nm, and the generation rate of O2·- was expressed as μmol kg-1 min-1.

The pulverized snap bean pod tissues were homogenized in sodium acetate buffer (100 mmol L-1, pH 5.5) containing 1 mmol L-1 polyethylene glycol (PEG), 4% polyvinyl-polypyrrolidone (PVPP) and 1% Triton X-100 in an ice bath for peroxidase (POD) and polyphenol oxidase (PPO) extraction. The POD activity was determined as described by Ali et al. (2022) with minor modifications. The enzyme extract was mixed with 3.0 mL of 25 mmol L-1 guaiacol solution. Then, 0.2 mL of 0.5 mol L-1 H2O2 solution was added to the mixture to initiate the reaction. The change in absorbance of the reaction mixture was recorded at 470 nm. PPO activity was determined by adding enzyme extract after mixing 4.0 mL of 50 mmol L-1 sodium acetate buffer (pH 5.5) with 1.0 mL of 50 mmol L-1 catechol solution. The increase in absorbance of the reaction mixture was recorded at 420 nm. A unit of POD/PPO activity was equal to a change of 1.0 in the absorbance value per minute and was expressed as U kg-1.

The extraction of all crude enzymes from snap bean pods was conducted while samples were immersed in an ice bath. The buffers used to extract the various antioxidant enzymes were as follows: (1) SPB (100 mmol L-1, pH 7.8) containing 5 mmol L-1 dithiothreitol (DTT) and 5% polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) was used to extract superoxide dismutase (SOD) and catalase (CAT); (2) potassium phosphate buffer (100 mmol L-1, pH 7.5) containing 0.1 mmol L-1 ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA), 1 mmol L-1 ascorbic acid and 2% PVPP was used to extract ascorbate peroxidase (APX).

SOD activity was determined according to the method of Ali et al. (2016). The reaction mixture contained 1.7 mL SPB (50 mmol L-1), 0.3 mL methionine (MET, 130 mmol L-1), 0.3 mL nitroblue tetrazolium (NBT, 750 μmol L-1), 0.3 mL EDTA-Na2 (100 μmol L-1), 0.3 mL riboflavin (20 μmol L-1) and 0.1 mL enzyme extract. A unit of SOD activity was equal to 50% inhibition of NBT photoreduction at 560 nm; SOD activity was expressed as U kg-1.

CAT activity was determined by adapting the protocol of Fang et al. (2016). The enzymatic reaction system consisted of 2.9 mL H2O2 (20 mmol L-1) and 0.1 mL crude enzyme extract. The CAT activity was expressed as U kg-1 after observing the change in absorbance at 240 nm in 0.5-min intervals within 3 min. A unit of CAT activity was equal to a reduction of 0.01 in the absorbance per minute.

APX activity was measured using an assay as described by Nakano and Asada (1981). The assay mixture contained 2.6 mL reaction buffer (50 mmol L-1 SPB, including 0.1 mmol L-1 EDTA and 0.5 mmol L-1 ascorbic acid) and 0.1 mL enzyme extract. The enzymatic reaction was initiated by adding 0.3 mL H2O2 (2.0 mmol L-1), and the decrease in absorbance of the mixture was observed at 290 nm in 1-min intervals for 6 min. A unit of APX activity was equal to a decline of 1.0 in the absorbance per minute; the APX activity was expressed as U kg-1.

Glutathione reductase (GR) activity was determined with a GR detection kit (Solarbio Science and Technology Co., Ltd., Beijing, China) according to the manufacturer’s instructions. A unit of GR activity was defined as the amount of enzyme required to oxidize 1.0 μmol nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate (NADPH) per minute, and changes in absorbance were recorded at 340 nm; the GR activity was expressed as U kg-1.

The AsA content in snap bean pods was determined using the 4,7-diphenyl-1,10-phenanthroline colorimetric method, as described by Arakawa et al. (1981) with slight modification. Snap bean pod tissue (10 g) was ground with 20 mL of TCA (5%) under ice bath conditions and diluted to 100 mL with 5% TCA, followed by extraction for 10 min. The homogenate was filtered, and the filtrate was collected. The AsA assay mixture consisted of 1.0 mL sample extract, 1.0 mL TCA (5%), 1.0 mL anhydrous ethanol, 0.5 mL phosphoric acid-ethanol (0.4%), 1.0 mL BP-ethanol (0.5%) and 0.5 mL FeCl3-ethanol (0.03%) solution. The absorbance of the reaction system was monitored at 534 nm and the results were expressed as mg kg-1.

Assay kits from Nanjing Jiancheng Bioengineering Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China) were used to measure GSH content in snap bean pods. Briefly, snap bean pod tissues were homogenized under ice bath conditions and centrifuged for 10 min at 4 °C. The absorbance value of the supernatant was recorded at 412 nm, and the results were expressed as mg kg-1.

The changes in oxygen and carbon dioxide concentrations inside the different snap bean packages throughout the storage period are shown in Fig. 1A-B. During the initial phase of the storage period, a sharp decrease in O2 content corresponded to a rapidly increasing concentration of CO2. This phenomenon can be attributed to the vigorous respiration and high metabolic rate of postharvest snap beans, resulting in O2 consumption (Fig. 1A) and CO2 production (Fig. 1B). After six days, the changes in oxygen and carbon dioxide levels inside the packages tended to be stable and reached dynamic equilibrium. From day six to day fifteen, the CO2 content inside the CC package was 3.53–3.97%, and the O2 content was 16.17–16.70%. The CO2 concentration inside the CCPF group was 4.45–4.80%, and the O2 concentration fluctuated from 14.02% to 14.33%.

According to previous reports, the photocatalytic degradation performance of ethylene by the CCPF was evaluated by degradation kinetics (Wang et al., 2019; Xie et al., 2020). The kinetic equation of ethylene photodegradation can be described using the Langmuir–Hinshelwood model and analyzed by the equation ln (Ct/C0) = -k·t (k, t, C0 and Ct represent the reaction rate constant, irradiation time, initial ethylene concentration, and ethylene concentration at time t, respectively). As shown in Fig. S2, compared with CCF, the ethylene degradation curve of CCPF conformed to a pseudofirst-order reaction (R 2 =0.998), which directly reflected the degradation effect of CCPF on ethylene under visible light. The reaction rate constant k value of ethylene photodegradation for CCPF reached 9.33 × 10-4 min-1, indicating that CCPF has excellent photodegradation performance for ethylene under visible light.

To verify the ethylene degradation ability of CCPF, the headspace gas concentrations of ethylene inside different packages were measured. As shown in Fig. 1C, in the first 3 d of storage, the ethylene concentration exhibited a rapid upward trend. The increase in ethylene concentration was mainly related to the higher oxygen concentration inside the package at the initial stage of storage, which promoted postharvest snap bean respiration and led to a large amount of ethylene production. After storage for 6 d, the changes in ethylene levels in both packages decreased slightly and then plateaued. Notably, the CCF group showed a significant peak on day six of storage, which was related to the respiratory peak of the snap beans.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Respiration rate of the snap beans after different packaging treatments
ce:section.ce:para: Increases in respiration can promote the ripening and senescence of snap beans, which seriously affects postharvest storage quality. As shown in Fig. 2A, the respiration rate of the snap beans in the control and CCF groups peaked on day six and then decreased. Compared with the unpackaged snap beans, the increase in the respiration rate of CCF-packaged snap beans was less rapid. Interestingly, the CCPF group had no apparent respiration peak and showed a lower respiration rate than the other two groups throughout the storage period. After 15 d of storage, the respiration rate of the snap beans in the CCPF group was 35.14% and 19.10% lower than that in the control and CCF groups, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: WL and firmness of snap beans after different packaging treatments
ce:section.ce:para: The changes in WL reflect water loss during storage. As shown in Fig. 2B, unpackaged snap beans lost weight over the entire storage period due to respiration and moisture evaporation within the snap beans. In contrast, less WL occurred in the CCF and CCPF-packaged snap beans, with the CCPF-packaged snap beans exhibiting the lowest WL (11.00%). The results showed that the packaging, especially CCPF packaging, effectively limited water loss from the surface of the snap bean pods.

Pod hardness is an important measure of snap bean quality during storage, with decreased pod hardness negatively affecting commercial value. The hardness of snap bean pods in all groups gradually decreased with the extension of time (Fig. 2C). The firmness of snap bean pods decreased to 6.46 N and 8.85 N for the control and CCF groups at the end of the storage period, respectively; however, the firmness of the snap bean pods packaged with the CCPF was higher than both groups at 9.58 N. The snap bean pods in the unpacked group exhibited a sharp decrease in hardness, which could be associated with their rapid weight loss (Paulsen et al., 2022). It has also been reported that the decrease in hardness could be related to tissue softening caused by cell wall material damage (Yang et al., 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual appearance and microstructural changes of snap bean pods after different packaging treatments
ce:section.ce:para: Visual appearance is considered an important parameter affecting the acceptability of snap beans. The appearance quality of different treated snap beans during storage is expressed as Fig. 3. Freshly harvested snap beans exhibit a high sensory quality and are green, appear full and lustrous. The unpackaged snap beans senesced and deteriorated rapidly during storage and began to wilt and decay on the day nine. In contrast, CCF and CCPF packaging effectively maintained the appearance quality of snap beans under the same storage conditions, with CCPF packaging resulting in superior quality. The snap bean samples packaged with pure CCF deteriorated after 12 d of storage. These results indicate that CCF and CCPF packaging effectively maintains the apparent quality of snap beans.

The microstructures of the snap bean pods were determined before and after storage. Before storage, the fresh snap bean pod cells were intact, plump and dense ( Fig. 4A). After storage for 15 d, the control snap bean pods showed severely distorted and damaged cells, and the intercellular intervals were larger (Fig. 4B). In contrast, the CCF and CCPF groups maintained better cell structures (Fig. 4C and D). Notably, the cells of the snap bean pods in the CCPF group were relatively plump, while the regional structures of the pod cells packaged by CCF were still slightly shrunken. These results showed that CCPF packaging maintained the micromorphologies and intercellular intervals of the snap bean pods more effectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TSS, TA, SPC and TCC of snap bean pods after different packaging treatments
ce:section.ce:para: TSS is a vital indicator of the edible quality in harvested snap beans. As shown in Fig. 5A, the TSS of control samples increased and then gradually decreased with storage time. The TSS in all groups peaked at 6 d, with the highest values observed in the control group (8.37%). The TSS in the CCF and CCPF groups were 7.83% and 7.73%, respectively. After storage for 15 d, samples in CCPF packaging had the highest (6.63%) and unpackaged samples had the lowest (5.80%) TSS value.

TA directly affects snap bean pod flavor, taste, and edible quality. Fig. 5B presents changes in the TA content of snap bean pods with different treatments. The TA content of snap bean pods in the control group decreased by 0.42% over the 15 d storage period. In contrast, the TA content of the CCPF-packaged snap bean pods decreased at a lower rate than that of the control and CCF-packaged samples. In particular, the CCPF-packaged samples showed the lowest decline in TA content, indicating that a higher TA content was retained in snap bean pods over the entire storage period; thus, CCPF packaging maintained better flavor quality in snap bean pods.

SPC is involved in various physiological and biochemical metabolic processes and is an indicator of nutritional value in snap beans. As shown in Fig. 5C, the SPC of snap bean pods in each group continuously declined during the storage period. The SPC decreased by 62.80% in the unpacked group after 15 d of storage, which was higher than that of the CCF and CCPF groups. At the end of the storage period, the CCPF-packaged snap beans maintained a relatively high SPC (5.56 g kg-1).

Chlorophyll is an essential pigment for plant photosynthesis. A large amount of chlorophyll exists in snap bean pods, and changes in concentration are indicative of the freshness and edible quality of postharvest snap beans. As shown in Fig. 5D, the TCC of snap bean pods decreased gradually during storage in both the packaged and control groups. The TCC of the control groups rapidly decreased in the first 6 d of storage, while that in the CCPF group decreased slowly. After fifteen days, CCPF-packaged snap bean pods had 1.12-fold and 1.56-fold higher TCC values than CCF-packaged and unpackaged snap bean pods, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Membrane permeability and MDA content of snap bean pods after different packaging treatments
ce:section.ce:para: The results of RC testing in samples from the control and packaging groups are summarized in Fig. 6A. The RC of snap bean pods in the control group rose from 17.80% to 47.22% during the storage period, whereas the RC of snap bean pods in the CCF and CCPF groups peaked at 40.54% and 36.32%, respectively. The RC of snap bean pods in the CCPF group remained higher than that of the CCF and control groups from day three to day fifteen.

The MDA content of each group was monitored during 15 d of storage (Fig. 6B). During the first 3 d, the MDA content in the unpackaged and CCF-packaged snap bean pods increased and continued to rise until day fifteen to 7.28 μmol kg-1 and 5.07 μmol kg-1, respectively. In contrast, the MDA content in the CCPF-packaged snap bean pods increased slowly throughout the storage process, from 0.60 μmol kg-1 on day 0–3.90 μmol kg-1 on day fifteen. It was evident that the snap beans in the CCPF group accumulated less MDA than beans in the control and CCF groups, revealing a dramatic reduction in membrane lipid peroxide damage when snap bean pods were packaged with CCPF material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: H2O2 content and O2·- generation rate of snap bean pods after different packaging treatments
ce:section.ce:para: The H2O2 content and O2·- generation rate are vital indicators of ROS accumulation and oxidative damage in snap bean pods during postharvest storage. Excessive accumulation of O2·- and H2O2 could result in an imbalance in reactive oxygen metabolism and adversely affect cells. As expected, the O2·- generation rate and H2O2 content of all snap bean pods gradually increased overall during storage. As shown in Fig. 6C, the O2·- generation rate of the unpackaged snap bean pods increased during the first 3 d, but lower O2·- production levels were observed in both packaged groups, with no difference between the two. At the end of the storage period, the O2·- generation rate in the CCPF group was lower by 43.97% and 17.55% than that in the control and CCF groups, respectively. The H2O2 content in the different treatment groups during storage was also determined (Fig. 6D). Similar to the trends observed in the O2·- generation rate, H2O2 content in control snap bean pods increased from 0 d to 15 d, whereas H2O2 accumulation was inhibited in snap bean pods packaged with CCF and CCPF. After storage for 15 d, the CCPF group had the lowest H2O2 content (166.38 mmol kg-1) and the unpacked group had the highest (262.76 mmol kg-1) content. These results suggest that CCF and CCPF packaging effectively reduced the O2·- generation rate and H2O2 accumulation in snap bean pods throughout the storage period and mitigated ROS damage compared to unpackaged snap bean pods, with CCPF packaging exhibiting greater effectiveness.

ce:section.ce:section-title: POD, PPO, SOD, CAT, APX and GR activities of snap bean pods after different packaging treatments
ce:section.ce:para: The dynamic changes in POD and PPO activities were associated with the oxidation resistance and enzymatic browning of postharvest snap bean pods (Ali et al., 2023). As shown in Fig. 7A, POD activity in all snap bean pod samples increased slightly during the first 3 d of storage, and POD activity was the same between the two packaging groups. After six days, POD activity in unpackaged snap bean pods increased sharply but increased slowly in CCF- and CCPF-packaged samples. The POD activity of snap bean pods in the CCF and CCPF groups was lower than that in the control group, except during the first 3 d of storage. After 15 d of storage, the snap bean pods in the CCPF group had the lowest POD activity (71.18 × 103 U kg-1) among all groups. The PPO activity of snap bean pods stored under different conditions was determined; the results are shown in Fig. 7B. On day six of the storage period, the PPO activity of snap bean pods in the control, CCF and CCPF groups increased from 79.84 U kg-1 to 363.08, 459.71 and 440.53 U kg-1, respectively, with the highest PPO activity observed in the CCF group, although differences in PPO activity between the two packaging groups were not significant. From day nine to day fifteen, the PPO activity of snap bean pods in the control group increased rapidly and was higher than that of the CCF and CCPF groups. The snap bean pods in the control group exhibited the highest PPO activity at the end of storage, which was 1.35- and 1.53-fold higher than that of the CCF and CCPF groups, respectively. The above results indicated that CCPF packaging effectively inhibited the activities of POD and PPO and reduced the oxidation of polyphenols and the formation of quinones, thus inhibiting the enzymatic browning that occurs in postharvest snap bean pods.

SOD is an enzyme that contains metal prosthetic groups and serves as the first defense against ROS by scavenging O2·-, thereby mitigating damage induced by ROS on the cell membrane system (Yu et al., 2022). As shown in Fig. 7C, the SOD activity of all snap bean pods generally increased during the initial phase of storage and then decreased throughout the remainder of the storage period. Notably, the SOD activity of snap bean pods in the control group peaked on day six, while the CCF and CCPF groups all reached the maximum SOD activity on day nine. During the first 6 d of storage, the SOD activity of snap bean pods in the control group increased rapidly and was higher than that of the two packaging groups. With the extension of storage time, the SOD activity of the control group decreased to a level lower than that of the packaging group. After 15 d of storage, the snap bean pods in the CCPF group showed the highest SOD activity, which was 2.01- and 1.21-fold higher than those in the control and CCF groups, respectively. These results indicated that CCPF packaging effectively improved and maintained the SOD activity of snap bean pods during storage, thus enhancing the ability to scavenge O2·- and contributing to a delay in the senescence of postharvest snap beans.

Alterations in the CAT activity of snap bean pods during the storage period were investigated (Fig. 7D). In general, the CAT activity of all snap bean pods peaked and then declined during the storage period. CAT activity was not different between the groups during the first 3 d of storage. The CAT activity of snap bean pods in the CCPF group peaked at 93.51 × 103 U kg-1 at 6 d, which was higher than that of the CCF group (85.47 × 103 U kg-1) and the control group (78.81 × 103 U kg-1). After 15 d of storage, the CAT activity of snap bean pods in the CCPF group was 1.43- and 1.14-fold higher than that in the unpackaged and CCF groups, respectively. The results demonstrated that CCPF packaging enhanced the CAT activity of snap bean pods during storage and improved the ability to catalyze the decomposition of H2O2, thus reducing the oxidative damage caused by H2O2.

APX is a highly specific peroxidase that catalyzes the decomposition of H2O2 with ascorbic acid serving as an electron donor. It mainly exists in chloroplasts, mitochondria and microbodies of snap bean pods. The APX activity of snap bean pods in each group increased until day nine of storage and then decreased over the remainder of the storage period (Fig. 7E). In the first 3 d of storage, APX activity increased in snap bean pods in the CCF and CCPF groups relative to bean pods in the control group and there was no difference in APX activity in the packaged snap bean pods. The maximum APX activity of snap bean pods in the CCPF group was 111.83 U kg-1 on the day nine, which was 21.44% and 47.38% higher than snap bean pods in the CCF and control groups, respectively. The CCPF-packaged snap bean pods showed higher APX activity than the other samples throughout storage. After 15 d of storage, the snap bean pods in the CCPF group continued to exhibit higher APX activity at 85.78 U kg-1 of, which was 1.38- and 2.03-fold higher than that of the CCF and control groups, respectively. These results suggest that CCPF packaging enhances the APX activity of snap bean pods to a certain extent, thus accelerating the catalytic decomposition of H2O2 and protecting the snap bean pod tissues from oxidative damage.

As a flavinase, GR is localized in various plant tissues to maintain sufficient cellular concentrations of reduced glutathione (GSH). As shown in Fig. 7F, the GR activity of snap bean pods increased until day six and progressively decreased after day six regardless of packaging. The activity of GR increased at a higher rate in the CCPF-packaged snap bean pods in comparison with those in the CCF and control groups. On day six, the GR activity of the CCPF-packaged snap bean pods was 1.14- and 1.30-fold higher than that of the CCF-packaged and unpackaged samples, respectively. After 15 d of storage, the GR activity of snap bean pods in the CCPF, CCF and control groups was 46.95, 38.81 and 22.76 U kg-1, respectively. The snap bean pods in the CCPF group maintained the highest GR activity, which was higher than that in the other groups. Compared with the control and CCF-packaged snap bean pods, the GR activity of the CCPF-packaged snap bean pods was higher during the entire storage process. These results demonstrate that CCPF packaging treatment improves the GR activity of snap bean pods, thus maintaining the normal function of the protein as well as the high reduction potential in cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: AsA and GSH content of snap bean pods after different packaging treatments
ce:section.ce:para: As a nonenzymatic antioxidant system, the AsA-GSH cycle plays a crucial role in scavenging ROS produced by snap bean pod cells (Zhu et al., 2019). The AsA content of snap bean pods in each group continued to decrease over the entire storage period, and the decrease in the control group was greater compared with the packaging groups ( Fig. 8A). The AsA content of snap bean pods in the control group rapidly decreased over the first three days of storage and was lower than that in the packaging groups. With the extension of storage time, snap bean pods in the CCPF group had a higher AsA content than those in the CCF group. After 15 d of storage, the AsA content was highest in the CCPF group (138.50 mg kg-1) and lowest in the control group (71.70 mg kg-1). On the other hand, the GSH content of all snap bean pods increased from 0 d to 6 d and then decreased thereafter (Fig. 8B). The maximum GSH content in CCPF snap bean pods occurred at day six, which was 1.06- and 1.18-fold higher than that of the CCF and control groups. The GSH content decreased as pod storage time increased. A comparison of each group at the end of the storage period showed that the GSH content of snap bean pods in the CCPF group was the highest (53.75 mg kg-1), while that in the control group was the lowest (27.54 mg kg-1) with different GSH contents presented in each of the three groups.

Pearson’s correlation analysis was conducted to examine various parameters in this study and the results are shown in Fig. 9 A. Both positive and negative correlations were found. Respiration rate, firmness, TSS, TA, SPC and TCC positively correlated with AsA content, GSH content, CAT and GR activities and negatively correlated with weight loss, RC, MDA content, H2O2 content, O2·- generation rate, and the activities of SOD, APX, POD and PPO. There was positive correlation between weight loss, RC, MDA content, H2O2 content and O2·- generation rate. Notably, RC, MDA content, H2O2 content and O2·- generation rate positively correlated with POD and PPO activities and negatively correlated with CAT activity, GR activity and the antioxidants GSH and AsA. In addition, the MDA and H2O2 content correlated negatively with firmness, TA, SPC and TCC.

Hierarchical cluster analysis (HCA) of snap beans after different treatments during storage based on the Euclidean correlation is presented in Fig. 9B. In cluster 1, all snap bean samples exhibited a continuous upward trend in weight loss, RC, MDA content, H2O2 content, O2·- generation rate, and POD and PPO activities. In cluster 2, all samples showed a loss in firmness, TCC, SPC, TA, TSS, AsA and GSH content after 15 d of storage. In addition, all samples showed a trend of increasing and then decreasing SOD, APX, CAT and GR activities during the storage period. These findings demonstrate that postharvest senescence processes were unfavorable to the overall quality of snap beans. It is noteworthy that the CCPF-packaged samples possessed higher nutritional quality (TSS, TA, SPC and TCC), antioxidant content (GSH and AsA), antioxidant enzyme activities (APX, SOD, CAT and GR) and lower oxidative damage (RC, MDA content, H2O2 content and O2·- generation rate) than samples in the other groups after 15 d of storage, which indicates that CCPF packaging is most effective for extending the shelf life of snap bean pods. Altogether, these results suggest a plausible mechanism by which CCPF packaging treatment prevents oxidative damage and maintains postharvest quality in snap bean pods ( Fig. 10).

Snap beans maintain vigorous life activity after harvesting and are prone to loss of quality and deterioration. Packaging with active films can maintain the storage quality of snap beans and extend their shelf life. In this study, a CS/CMC-based photodynamic packaging material that contained novel metal-free carbon dot/g-C3N4 nanocomposite photosensitizers was prepared and applied to delay the quality deterioration of snap beans. The photodynamic packaging treatments were effective in delaying postharvest senescence and maintaining the overall quality of snap beans during storage.

An optimal concentration of oxygen and carbon dioxide inside packaging is essential to maintain food freshness. During the entire storage period, the CCPF group maintained lower O2 concentrations and higher CO2 concentrations than those of the CCF group, indicating that the CCPF packaging treatment created an internal atmosphere that promoted freshness. Possible reasons to explain this are as follows: First, the CCPF has better gas barrier properties, which can prevent O2 outside the package from penetrating in and reduce the discharge of CO2 generated by respiration. Second, the CN photosensitizer incorporated in the CCPF was activated by visible light irradiation and consumed some oxygen inside the CCPF package to generate active species with antibacterial and ethylene degradation functions. Although CCPF had better gas barrier properties, the CCPF group showed lower ethylene levels than the CCF group during the entire storage period. It is plausible that CCPF degraded the ethylene produced during the storage of snap beans. The ethylene degradation test also directly confirmed the photodegradation effect of CCPF on ethylene. These findings were similar to those of Xie et al. (2022), who reported that the Ag2O-TiO2-Bi2WO6/PVA composite film effectively prevented the respiration of bananas by degrading ethylene. Moreover, the decrease in ethylene production in snap beans might also be related to the influence of the CCPF on the metabolic pathway. Our previous studies confirmed the visible-light-driven antimicrobial ability of CCPF and elucidated the photodynamic antibacterial mechanism of CCPF. Based on the above studies on ethylene photodegradation and previous reports, a possible mechanism of CCPF during the photocatalytic degradation of ethylene was reasonably proposed (Gong et al., 2023):
(5) g-C3N4 + hv → g-C3N4 (e− + h+)
(6) g-C3N4 (e−) + CPCDs → g-C3N4 + CPCDs (e−)
(7) g-C3N4 (e−) + O2 → g-C3N4 + O2·-
(8) CPCDs (e−) + O2 → CPCDs + O2·-
(9) C2H4 + O2·- → CO2 + H2O

Respiration rate, WL, firmness, TSS, TA, SPC and TCC are important indicators of physiology and quality that should be monitored during snap bean storage. In this study, CCPF packaging effectively inhibited the respiration rate of postharvest snap beans, possibly due to the formation of a controlled atmosphere microenvironment with a low concentration of O2 and a high concentration of CO2 inside the packages, which inhibited the respiratory metabolism and transpiration of snap beans (Wang et al., 2021). In addition, CCPF packaging may have also weakened respiration by reducing the activity of respiration-related enzymes (Tan et al., 2021). The reduction in WL was mainly due to the excellent physical barrier and water resistance properties of the CCPF. After incorporating CN nanocomposites, the hydrophobicity of the blended polysaccharide films increased, and the water vapor barrier performance was enhanced (Chi et al., 2019). The reduction in the content of hydrophilic groups in the polymer matrix created tortuous micropaths for water molecules, forcing water vapors to diffuse through the CCPF through an extended path (Liu et al., 2017). In addition, the effective maintenance of nutrients in snap beans by the CCPF treatment also contributes to the reduction in weight loss. Snap bean pods packaged with CCPF had the lowest softening rate due to the photodegradation of ethylene by the CCPF, which resulted in less ethylene accumulation inside the package, since tissue softening is sensitive to ethylene; this observation is corroborated by the values of ethylene concentration measured inside the packages. In addition, sustained high concentrations of carbon dioxide in the internal atmosphere of the packages also inhibited the activity of the cell wall hydrolase enzymes that contribute to softened bean pod tissue (Yuan et al., 2023).

The increase in TSS during the ripening stage of snap beans could be related to the hydrolysis of starch and other macromolecular polysaccharides (Kaewklin et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2022). The extended storage time may have enabled the respiration of snap beans to continue, resulting in the consumption of nutrients such as organic acids and sugars and a decrease in TSS (Wang et al., 2022). Compared with the control and CCF packaging groups, a delay in the ripening process was observed in the CCPF packaging group through the inhibition of ethylene production and respiration. Therefore, drastic changes in TSS were prevented, and a higher TSS level was maintained in snap bean pods. In addition, the sustained TA in snap bean pods packed with CCPF could be related to a reduction in the degradation of pectin molecules (de Matos Fonseca et al., 2021). Indeed, de Matos Fonseca et al. (2021) reported that papayas treated with gelatin-TiO2-coated nanocomposite foam nets also maintain a high TA value after three days of storage. The reason for SPC retention may be that the CCPF packaging treatment inhibited the physiological and metabolic activities of snap bean pods, thus reducing the decomposition and utilization of soluble protein. A similar delay in SPC decline was found by Yan et al. (2023), who reported that biocomposite films containing mesoporous nanosilica reduced the consumption of SPC in mushrooms. The results of TCC testing suggest that snap bean pods in the CCPF group maintained a high chlorophyll content during the entire storage period, which could be due to the reduction in chlorophyll decomposition and carotenoid accumulation in snap beans after the CCPF packaging treatment, thereby alleviating yellowing and wilting (Amorim et al., 2023). Similar results were found by Zhu et al. (2023), who reported that LDPE/SF/5ZnO film effectively inhibited decreases in chlorophyll content in lettuce during storage.

ROS are oxygen-containing chemically active substances produced by plants when electrons are transferred to oxygen molecules during aerobic metabolism. Studies have shown that ROS metabolism imbalance adversely affects postharvest vegetable senescence (Zhang et al., 2019; Ma et al., 2019). The production and elimination of ROS in snap bean pod cells helped to maintain a dynamic equilibrium state with low levels of ROS, which avoided oxidative damage to the cells (Tian et al., 2013). However, as senescence proceeds, the balance of ROS metabolism in postharvest snap bean pod cells is disrupted, resulting in ROS accumulation, which induces cellular dysregulation and accelerates cellular senescence (Wang et al., 2019). This process was usually accompanied by an increase in the O2·- generation rate, H2O2 content, membrane lipid peroxidation and membrane permeability. RC is an essential indicator of membrane permeability since it reflects the integrity and function of the cell membrane. MDA is one of the main products of membrane lipid peroxidation in the cell membrane system of snap bean pods, and its accumulation indicates the extent of membrane peroxidation (Kaewklin et al., 2018). In our present work, the different packaging treatments, especially CCPF packaging, delayed increases in RC and accumulation in MDA to varying degrees, which helped to maintain bean pod membrane integrity during storage. This may be because CCPF effectively delayed the ripening and senescence of snap bean pods by reducing the respiration rate, which inhibits the occurrence of electrolyte extravasation and membrane lipid oxidation. reduces damage to the cell membrane (Li et al., 2022). Notably, preventing oxidative stress, which reduced snap bean pod exposure to ROS, also protected cell membranes from damage (Wang et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2021). Indeed, other studies have shown that chitosan-cinnamon essential oil/sodium alginate-TiO2 bilayer films inhibit ROS generation in mangoes, thereby reducing MDA accumulation (Wang et al., 2022). In addition, the H2O2 content and O2·- generation rate in the CCPF packaging treatment were lower throughout the storage period. This phenomenon may be explained by the fact that CCPF packaging effectively enhanced the activities of antioxidant enzymes (SOD, CAT, APX and GR) and maintained ROS balance in cells, which decreased the H2O2 content and O2·- generation rate (Yang et al., 2021).

Cellular defense mechanisms against oxidative stress in unpackaged and packaged snap bean pods were evaluated by investigating the activities of POD, PPO, SOD, CAT, APX and GR enzymes, which play an essential role in postharvest fruit senescence and free radical scavenging (Mittler, 2002). PPO can catalyze the oxidation of various simple phenols to form quinones. These quinone compounds can be further polymerized to form brown or black polymers. In addition, POD is an essential oxidoreductase in vegetables that can catalyze the reaction of H2O2 with phenolics to generate quinones (Sun et al., 2015). POD is in contact with phenolic compounds during membrane compartmentalization loss. Therefore, POD and PPO activity is closely related to the tissue browning of postharvest snap bean pods during storage (Ali et al., 2023). The O2·- generated in snap bean pods during storage may be converted into H2O2 and O2 by SOD, and the resulting H2O2 can be catalyzed by CAT and APX, eventually decomposing into H2O and O2, thereby reducing ROS-induced tissue damage (Li et al., 2022; Fang et al., 2016). CAT and APX may be responsible for scavenging excess ROS under stress and finely regulating ROS signaling due to their different affinities for H2O2, respectively (Mittler, 2002). In addition, GR works in concert with CAT and APX to detoxify H2O2 (Blokhina et al., 2003). In this work, CCPF packaging treatment conserved the higher membrane integrity of snap bean pods, which probably suppressed the loss of compartmentalization and inhibited the activity of POD and PPO enzymes, thus effectively inhibiting the surface browning of snap bean pods. This was consistent with the findings of Ali et al. (2023) and Yan et al. (2023). More importantly, CCPF packaging showed higher SOD, CAT, APX and GR activities in treated snap bean pods. The higher activity of these enzymes proved beneficial to improve the ability of snap bean pods to scavenge ROS, thereby maintaining the balance of ROS metabolism and delaying the quality deterioration of snap bean pods during storage.

Similarly, AsA-GSH is an imperative antioxidant defense system that helps in the detoxification of ROS in snap bean pods (Feng et al., 2022). In this system, AsA and GSH are two important nonenzymatic antioxidants where AsA can act as an electron donor for the catalytic decomposition of hydrogen peroxide through APX or by directly quenching ROS and GSH serves as a substrate to regenerate AsA through the reduction of monodehydroascorbic acid (MDHA) and dehydroascorbate (DHA) under the catalysis of monodehydroascorbate reductase (MDHAR) and dehydroascorbate reductase (DHAR) (Yu et al., 2022; Ma et al., 2019). In the current study, CCPF packaging delayed the depletion of AsA and maintained a high level of GSH in snap bean pods during storage, thereby maintaining the redox state of AsA and GSH. This maintenance of nonenzymatic antioxidant capacity could be because CCPF packaging induces AsA-GSH cycle-related enzymes by regulating respiratory metabolism, ensuring the normal functioning of metabolic pathways in the cycle (Li et al., 2022). Moreover, the low oxygen concentration inside the package may have protected AsA and GSH from oxidative loss.

According to the results of the correlation analysis, the postharvest senescence of snap beans was accompanied by a decrease in nutritional quality and an increase in ROS levels, thus confirming the close relationship between ROS metabolism and the deterioration in the postharvest quality of snap bean pods. To our knowledge, this is the first study on the relationship between postharvest quality deterioration and ROS metabolism in snap bean pods under photodynamic packaging treatment. This study found that the novel CCPF packaging treatment regulated ROS metabolism and enhanced the defense against oxidative damage compared with control and CCF packaging, thus effectively maintaining the storage quality of postharvest snap bean pods (Fig. 10). Compared with other film materials used for the preservation of snap beans, the photodynamic composite film used in this study was prepared based on carbon-based photosensitive materials and polysaccharides, showing persistent functional activity, responsibility and sustainability (Lucera et al., 2011; Kinyuru et al., 2011; Li et al., 2023). This photodynamic film is competent to meet the requirement of increasing demand of functional activity as storage time increases. In addition, this film can not only inhibit bacteria but also effectively prevent the occurrence of bacterial resistance because it does not require specific targeting interactions between photosensitizers and bacteria (Jia et al., 2019).

In summary, a novel CCPF packaging material effectively delayed deteriorations in the postharvest quality of snap beans over 15 d of storage. It reduced the respiration rate, weight loss and tissue softening in snap beans by creating an excellent physical barrier that enabled an optimal ratio of O2/CO2 and by degrading ethylene produced by the respiration of snap beans. CCPF packaging also maintained the TSS, TA, SPC and TCC of snap bean pods at a higher level than both unpackaged snap bean pods and those packaged with CCF material. Notably, CCPF packaging reduced oxidative damage caused by electrolyte leakage, MDA accumulation, H2O2 content and the O2·- generation rate in snap bean pods by regulating the two major (enzymatic and nonenzymatic) ROS scavenging systems. Furthermore, CCPF packaging reduced the degree of browning in snap bean pods by inhibiting the dramatic increase in POD and PPO activity. Pearson’s correlation analysis showed that the postharvest senescence of snap bean pods was closely related to ROS metabolism. CCPF packaging is a promising strategy to prevent quality deterioration in snap bean pods. This study offers a theoretical basis for exploring the preservation mechanism of postharvest snap beans and provides technical guidance for broadening the application of CCPF packaging for preserving other foods. Nevertheless, the molecular mechanism by which CCPF packaging regulates ROS metabolism in snap bean pods and the effect of CCPF packaging on bean grains need further investigation.

Dezhuang Gong: Conceptualization, Data curation, Methodology, Writing − original draft. Xinhua Zhang: Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation. Jing Zhang: Resources, Software. Jingyi Mu: Investigation, Resources. Xiaofeng Han: Validation, Visualization. Xiuling Zhang: Writing − review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Wentao Zhang: Writing − review & editing, Validation, Funding acquisition.
